methods.description: Procedures for preparing sediment samples and analyzing their geochemical composition, including organic carbon (C org), total nitrogen (TN), stable carbon isotopes (δ 13 C), and trace elements (Ni, Cu, Al). This involves sample treatment, elemental and isotopic measurements using EA-IRMS and XRF, normalization, and validation of analytical precision and accuracy.

steps.description: Twenty-four dry sediment samples (~0.5 cm each) ground in a porcelain mortar, were used for geochemical analyses at the ALY-SES platform (IRD/SU, Bondy France).
steps.searchable_summary: grind dry sediment samples

steps.description: The samples for C org and δ 13 C analysis were treated with HCl 10% for removal of the carbonate fraction.
steps.searchable_summary: treat sediment samples with HCl to remove carbonates

steps.description: The content of C org and TN and the stable carbon isotopic ratio 13 C/ 12 C of the organic matter were determined on a Flash HT 2000 elemental analyzer, coupled to an isotopic ratio mass spectrometer Delta Vplus via a combustion-ConFlow IV interface from Thermo Fischer Scientific. Bulk sediment was used for TN analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: determine Corg, TN, and δ13C using elemental analyzer and IRMS

steps.description: The stable carbon isotopic ratio 13 C/ 12 C of OM is reported in the conventional δ-notation with respect to the PDB (Pee Dee Belemnite) carbonate standard defined with the equation δ 13 13 12 13 12 13 12
steps.searchable_summary: calculate and report δ13C values in delta notation relative to PDB standard

steps.description: The analytical precision was determined by replicates of the certified reference materials and was always below 0.12% for C org and TN, and lower than ±0.2‰ for δ 13 C.
steps.searchable_summary: validate analytical precision for Corg, TN, and δ13C using reference materials
outputs: analytical precision metrics (for Corg, TN, δ13C)

steps.description: Ni, Cu, and Al concentrations were determined on 35 samples (~1 cm each) by X-ray fluorescence spectrometry (XRF, Spectrolab Xepos-3).
steps.searchable_summary: determine Ni, Cu, and Al concentrations using XRF

steps.description: Analytical precision was assessed through the replicate analysis (n = 3) of a single sediment sample, and the coefficients of variation were <0.6% for Ni and Cu, and <0.01% for Al).
steps.searchable_summary: assess analytical precision for Ni, Cu, and Al using replicate analysis
outputs: coefficient of variation (for Ni, Cu, Al)

steps.description: The accuracy of the measurements was evaluated through the analysis of the reference material IAEA-158 with results obtained within the reported certified values.
steps.searchable_summary: evaluate accuracy for Ni, Cu, and Al using reference material IAEA-158

steps.description: As the Ni and Cu concentration in the sediments can be affected by terrestrial input, we normalized them with Al concentrations (Calvert and Pedersen, 2007).
steps.searchable_summary: normalize Ni and Cu concentrations by Al concentration

methods.description: Procedures for preparing sediment samples for diatom analysis, including physical and chemical treatments to remove unwanted components, preparing microscope slides, identifying and counting diatom valves, and calculating relative abundances. Includes validation of counting precision.

steps.description: Ninety-two sediment samples (~0.4 cm average thickness and ~6 years temporal resolution average; Table S3) were obtained for diatom analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: obtain sediment samples for diatom analysis

steps.description: Laminae were visually recognized and separated using an x-ray digitalized acetate template.
steps.searchable_summary: visually recognize and separate sediment laminae

steps.description: The dry weight of each sediment lamina was registered for abundance calculations.
steps.searchable_summary: measure and record dry weight of sediment laminae

steps.description: Sediment samples (~0.2 to ~0.5 g dry weight) were added with HCl 10% to eliminate carbonates
steps.searchable_summary: treat sediment samples with HCl to remove carbonates

steps.description: ...and digested at ~80°C with H 2 O 2 30% and HNO 3 70% to remove organic matter.
steps.searchable_summary: digest sediment samples with H2O2 and HNO3 to remove organic matter

steps.description: Acids and salts were removed through several washes with distilled water, settled for at least 24 h, and the liquid was discarded.
steps.searchable_summary: wash digested samples with distilled water to remove acids and salts

steps.description: The samples were diluted to a standard volume (30 mL).
steps.searchable_summary: dilute washed samples to a standard volume

steps.description: For diatom slides, samples were homogenized and 200 µL aliquots were taken.
steps.searchable_summary: homogenize diluted samples and take aliquots for slides

steps.description: In most samples, dilution was needed owing to the large number of particles, these volumes were considered for the calculation of total abundance.
steps.searchable_summary: perform additional dilutions on aliquots if necessary and record volumes

steps.description: Aliquot s were placed on coverslips (18 mm diameter) and dried at room temperature.
steps.searchable_summary: place aliquots on coverslips and dry

steps.description: Slides were mounted in Naphrax resin (refraction index = 1.74).
steps.searchable_summary: mount coverslips on slides using Naphrax resin

steps.description: All diatoms were identified at the genera or species taxonomic level using Cupp (1943), Round et al. (1990), Moreno et al. (1996), Hasle and Syvertsen (1997), Hernández-Becerril et al. (2021), and specialized literatures.
steps.searchable_summary: identify diatoms taxonomically using reference literature

steps.description: The samples were counted by transects (Schrader and Gersonde, 1978), at least 500 valves per sample were identified under a light microscope (Nikon eclipse Ni) at 1000x magnification with Nomarski interference contrast.
steps.searchable_summary: count diatom valves per sample using transect method under microscope

steps.description: The precision of the diatom analysis was evaluated through the replicate analysis (n = 8) of diatom slides; the relative standard deviation was <8%.
steps.searchable_summary: evaluate diatom counting precision using replicate analysis

steps.description: The species composition is reported as relative abundance (%).
steps.searchable_summary: calculate and report diatom species composition as relative abundance

methods.description: Methodology for defining ecological assemblages based on diatom data and applying Constrained Incremental Sum of Squares (CONISS) analysis to identify statistically significant zones within the sediment core based on changes in diatom assemblages.

steps.description: According to the abundance of the main diatom species and their ecological affinities based on the literature, three broad ecological assemblages were adopted: cold-water and high productivity taxa, temperate to warm-water taxa, and warm-water and low productivity taxa (Figure 4).
steps.searchable_summary: define ecological assemblages based on diatom species abundance and literature

steps.description: Visual observation of the CONISS and the broken stick model (Fig. S1) suggest four significant zones (at the 12.1 of total sum of squares) in the studied core (Figure 4).
steps.searchable_summary: perform CONISS analysis on diatom abundance data

steps.description: Visual observation of the CONISS and the broken stick model (Fig. S1) suggest four significant zones (at the 12.1 of total sum of squares) in the studied core (Figure 4).
steps.searchable_summary: apply broken stick model to determine significant zones from CONISS results

steps.description: The classification on the first hierarchical level presents two significant diatom assemblage zones... The classification on the second level subdivides... Finally, a third level separates another zone... The lowest zone... was characterized by the predominance of... The zone from... showed a relative increase of... From... some... increased. The upper part... was characterized by high abundances of...
steps.searchable_summary: describe the characteristics of the identified zones based on diatom assemblage composition
outputs: description of zones and their characteristic assemblages

methods.description: Methodology for establishing the chronology of the sediment core by integrating 210Pb and 14C dating. This involves applying different dating models, validating results, calibrating radiocarbon ages, estimating the regional marine reservoir age (ΔR) with uncertainty, and constructing a final integrated Bayesian age-depth model.

steps.description: The 210 Pb chronology was established a) with the Constant Flux model (Sanchez-Cabeza and Ruiz-Fernández, 2012)...
steps.searchable_summary: establish 210Pb chronology using the Constant Flux model

steps.description: ...with uncertainties estimated through a Monte Carlo method with 10 6 simulations...
steps.searchable_summary: estimate 210Pb age uncertainties using Monte Carlo simulations

steps.description: ...and b) with a bayesian method (Aquino-López et al., 2018) by using the R package rplum (Blaauw et al., 2021)...
steps.searchable_summary: establish 210Pb chronology using a Bayesian method (rplum R package)

steps.description: Both chronologies were almost identical and were successfully validated with the 239,240Pu onset, which is a robust age-dating marker in regions far from nuclear testing grounds (Sanchez-Cabeza et al., 2021) ( Figure 2).
steps.searchable_summary: validate 210Pb chronologies using 239,240Pu onset as a marker

steps.description: Following common practice, the radiocarbon ages were calibrated with the Marine20 curve (Heaton et al., 2020)...
steps.searchable_summary: calibrate radiocarbon ages using the Marine20 calibration curve

steps.description: ...and corrected for a regional marine reservoir age (ΔR) of 456 ± 51 years (Berger et al., 1966)...
steps.searchable_summary: correct calibrated 14C ages for a regional marine reservoir age (Delta R)

steps.description: To estimate the local ΔR, we followed the method described by Reimer and Reimer (2017)...
steps.searchable_summary: estimate local marine reservoir age (Delta R) using the Reimer and Reimer (2017) method

steps.description: To calculate uncertainties, we used a Monte Carlo approach with the R language (R Core Team, 2021). The 210 Pb-derived ages were expressed as BP (before present; age BP = 1950 -calendar age) and produced 10 6 simulations of the 210 Pb age BP (pb210.bp) and the sample radiocarbon age (c14) following normal distributions. Simulated 210 Pb BP ages younger than 1950 were discarded as they could not be calibrated with the MARINE20 curve. Each pb210.bp simulation was reverse-calibrated with the MARINE20 curve (pb210.c14) by using the calBP.14C function of the IntCal package (Blaauw, 2022). Then, the ΔR simulations were determined as the difference between the sample radiocarbon age simulations (c14) and the 210 Pb reverse-calibrated age simulations (pb210.c14) as . Finally, ΔR and its uncertainty were determined as the mean and standard deviation of all simulations, respectively (ΔR = 247 ± 30 years, 1 sigma).
steps.searchable_summary: calculate local Delta R uncertainty using Monte Carlo simulation

steps.description: The integrated 210 Pb-14 C age-model was produced by a bayesian approach (Blaauw and Christen, 2011) with the rbacon R package (Blaauw et al., 2022).
steps.searchable_summary: produce integrated 210Pb-14C age-depth model using a Bayesian approach (rbacon R package)

steps.description: We used all 210 Pb-derived ages and the three radiocarbon 14 C ages beyond the validated 210 Pb chronology (sections below 20.0 cm), with the MARINE20 curve and the calculated ΔR.
steps.searchable_summary: input 210Pb ages, selected 14C ages, Marine20 curve, and calculated Delta R into the integrated age model
outputs: inputs configured for integrated age model

steps.description: The integrated age-model showed a satisfactory agreement between 210 Pb and 14 C ages, and a common and smooth trend (Figure 3).
steps.searchable_summary: evaluate the integrated age-depth model for agreement and trend
outputs: evaluation of model fit and trend

steps.description: The integrated 210 Pb and 14 C age model indicated that the base of the core reached ~1500 CE (Table S4; Figure 3).
steps.searchable_summary: determine the age of the core base from the integrated age-depth model

steps.description: The average sediment accumulation rate from ~1907 to ~2014 CE was 0.14 ± 0.07 cm/year; whereas for the older sediment, the sedimentation rate was of 0.08 ± 0.06 cm/year (Tables S1 to S2).
steps.searchable_summary: calculate average sediment accumulation rates for different periods from the age-depth model
